3	Stefan Georgi
MODULE 3	Mechanism Example - Fungus
Unique Mechanism
EXAMPLE
Fungus Relief Product
(00:02)
All right. We’re going to do another example of unique mechanism. This one’s
going to be the mechanism for a fungus relief product. This is one for a sales
letter I wrote a couple of years back. The letter never really saw the light of day
or had a bunch of traction, but I really like this one. The mechanism especially
is very cool, clear and concise, and a lot of things I liked about the offer. Let
me go ahead and show you. I’m just taking it directly from the sales letter, so
it’s easy for you. One thing I do remember, if you watched the previous video,
about the olive oil mechanism where I was about to make a point and I forgot
the point, I did remember what that point was, which was: when you write the
mechanism out, the best approach or idea is that you write it like you actually
are going to want to put it in the sales letter, once you have it.
(01:02)
Then, as you’re going to see, we just copy and paste this into the sales letter.
By doing that, you’ve already written a portion of your sales letter here on step
two of the RMBC method. With this being a fungus offer, we need to explain
the unique mechanism behind the problem, the unique mechanism behind
how they got fungus or why they haven’t been able to get rid of their fungus.
We’re talking specifically about foot fungus. Here we go with this. How do
we get infected with fungus in the first place? As you probably already know,
it’s pretty easy to get a fungal infection. Places like gyms, locker-rooms, hot-
tubs, saunas, steam rooms, and showers are all hot-beds for these types of
fungal infections, as are places that have warm and moist climates. To make
matters worse, the elderly and Type 2 diabetics are especially at risk of fungal
infections.
(01:55)
I’ll explain why in a moment. Plus, it’s not just warm and moist environments
where fungus can thrive. Many types of fungus, including things like athlete’s
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 11 --

4	Stefan Georgi
MODULE 3	Mechanism Example - Fungus
foot, jock itch and toenail fungus, can be contagious. There are forms of fungal
infections that can make their way to our body through the air we breathe and
the surfaces we touch, meaning that pretty much everyone at Earth is at risk
of a fungal infection. It should be “on Earth”, but that’s okay. What happens
once you come into contact with fungus? The short answer is that it starts
to spread. Fungus have long branches that are called hyphae. You can think
of them as being similar to the roots of a tree. Once infected, your fungal
infection feeds off your skin, your nails, or your hair, which in turn allows the
hyphae (or roots) to grow, spreading further and deeper, covering more of
the surface of your skin, hair, or nails, while also penetrating your skin and
becoming embedded. What’s even creepier is the fact that these hyphae are
self-replicating, which means that they can continue to reproduce and create
new fungal cells again and again.
(02:55)
All of which makes your fungal infection increasingly difficult to kill. Makes
sense? Good because this is important to understand why it’s so hard to kill
your fungal infection. One, the fungus continues to self-replicate, which means
it’s hard to kill all of it at once. Two, because the fungus burrows into your
nails, skin, and hair. Three, many surface treatments aren’t effective since
they don’t penetrate deep enough into the infected area. This explains why
so many people suffer from reoccurring fungus, by the way, because even
when their skin or nails clear up, there is still fungus buried deep inside, like a
ticking time bomb just waiting to explode. This also explains why folks with
poor circulation are more likely to get fungal infections, because your white
blood cells can kill foreign invaders like fungus, but when circulation is bad,
you don’t get as many white blood cells to the infected area and your fungal
infections keep digging deeper and deeper.
(03:47)
Actually, this last part about the circulation stuff, I’m not even entirely
convinced that it explains it. I think I put that in there because a lot of people
who have issues with foot and nail fungus also have poor circulation and
they’re diabetic, so I wanted to get it for that. I think it’s fine here. Really, the
big thing, the big unique mechanism behind the problem here, is that, because
of these hyphae, your fungus keeps branching out like the roots of a tree, they
keep growing and spreading, and it’s embedded. It’s not just a surface level
problem. Your fungus becomes embedded, so it doesn’t just spread further
and further, but it also goes under the skin. Because of that, it makes your
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

fungus infection difficult to kill, because even if you do a surface level thing,

deep enough into the infected area.

You’ll notice a couple of things here. You’ll notice that I do stop to sum up

explains why so many people suffer from reoccurring fungus, by the way,
because even when your skin or nails clear up, there’s still fungus very deep
inside, like a ticking time bomb waiting to explode. Again, that’s a huge part
of the mechanism of the problem there. The other thing I want to quickly

said in the research component, about how the average American reads at a
seventh grade reading level. Even where I talk about it being self-replicating,
let’s find this here. What’s even creepier is the fact that these hyphae are
self-replicating, which means that they continue to reproduce and create new
fungal cells again and again. I don’t just leave it at self-replicating. I explain
why that matters. I think that’s an important extra layer. This is the unique
mechanism behind the problem for this fungus example. Now we’re going to
look at the unique mechanism behind the solution.
(06:09)
In this sales letter the product we’re selling is something that has something
called undecylenic acid in it. You may remember from the research video that
went through the document me telling you the story about undecylenic acid
and how it was discovered during World War II and everything. In this sales
letter we’ve explained the broad discovery of undecylenic acid, but now we
need to convince them that this really can address their problems. This is
where the unique mechanism behind the solution comes into play. Let’s read
through it. I started wondering if undecylenic acid could be the key to treating
foot fungus today, so I began researching everything I could about undecylenic
acid. I was surprised to discover that many modern OTC products contain this
ingredient today.
(06:59)
Plus, through my research I also uncovered the reason why undecylenic acid is
so effective in treating fungus. It’s actually pretty simple. Remember what I told
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 11 --

6	Stefan Georgi
MODULE 3	Mechanism Example - Fungus
you earlier about fungal infections, how the fungus under your skin continues
to self-replicate and branch out further and further, while also burrowing
deep inside of you, meaning that even when you destroy a fungal infection
on the surface, it can still be embedded inside of you, where it simply starts
over again and continues to grow and spread? Research has now shown that
undecylenic acid prevents fungal cells (hyphae) from creating new branches,
which means that once you apply undecylenic acid to an infected area, the
fungus on your skin can no longer continue to replicate. As a result, once
you’ve treated your fungal infection with undecylenic acid, it slowly starts to
die off, restoring your skin to its clear and unblemished natural state.
(07:47)
In other words, adding undecylenic acid to your fungal infection is like cutting
off all the oxygen to a fire. Once you do that, the fire stops spreading, loses
strength, and eventually burns out. It’s the same thing with your fungal
infection. Once you add undecylenic acid, the fungal infection is starved of
the fuel it needs to spread and it stops burning and dies out. I have some
studies and proof here as well. This part is not as vital. I’m going to read it
to you, but I want to just stop here for a second because, again, really, what
we’ve told them, if the problem is that you’ve got fungus that’s spreading and
branching out and getting embedded and it goes deeper and everything like
that, we’re saying that the solution is that undecylenic acid stops the fungus
from self-replicating.
(08:34)
You’ll notice that, by the way, even in this solution here I remind them of the
unique mechanism of the problem. Remember when I told you earlier about
fungal infections. I have that because, if I tell them that once and then start
talking about undecylenic acid, there’s a good chance I’ll have people who don’t
fully remember what I told them about the unique mechanism of the problem.
It’s important for me to remind them of that before I get to the solution. Again,
it’s that undecylenic acid prevents fungal cells from creating new branches,
which means that once you apply undecylenic acid to an infected area, the
fungus under your skin can no longer continue to replicate. That’s the unique
mechanism of the solution right there. It dies off and restores your skin.
(09:17)
We have a metaphor about how undecylenic acid, adding it to your fungal
infection is like cutting off the oxygen to a fire. We’re using a metaphor to

7	Stefan Georgi
MODULE 3	Mechanism Example - Fungus
make it easier for them to understand. Again, unique mechanism of the
problem is fungus branching out and continue to branch, and then burrowing
deep inside. Unique mechanism of the solution is that undecylenic acid stops
the branching, so the fungus shrivels up and dies, or it smoulders out, just
like if you cut off the oxygen to a fire make. Makes sense, right? Here is some
research that I put to further support that point of the solution. I’ll read that to
you. It’s less important than what I just said, but it’s worth looking at. I should
mention that this isn’t just a theory.
(10:04)
By the way. There’s actually a ton of research showing that undecylenic acid
can treat even severe cases of foot fungus and that it helps with ringworm,
jock itch, and even nail fungus too. This includes the 2001 study, which found
that undecylenic acid is 21% more effective than the next best treatment
for foot fungus, and another study from 2008 where 154 patients with mild,
moderate, and severe nail fungus disease used undecylenic acid regularly for
an extended period of time, and it was found that undecylenic acid was a
100% effective in eliminating mild nail fungus, 65% effective in eliminating
moderate nail fungus, and 35% effective in eliminate even the most severe
cases of nail fungus. In fact, undecylenic acid is even approved by the FDA
who wrote explicitly that people selling undecylenic acid can say that it can be
used for the cure of most athlete’s foot, jock itch, and ringworm, that it provides
effective relief from the itching, scaling, cracking, burning, and chafing.
(10:55)
Associated with jock itch, that it provides relief from the itchy scaly skin
between the toes and itching burning feet, and it’s even been proven effective
in the prevention of most forms of athlete’s foot with daily use. This is just
more proof of undecylenic acid. What’s cool here is this is, as you’ll see from
the citation, from the FDA’s website of what you can say around claims for it.
I took their guidelines, and putting them explicitly in here just adds a ton of
proof. That’s why I did it like that. I’m hoping you’re starting to really understand
the unique mechanism behind the problem and the solution now. We’ve done
two, the olive oil one and the fungus one. I’ll probably do a couple more for
you as well.
